edge: RS CMOS sensors capture high resolution images but introduce distortions and artifacts in the presence of motion.
Recent methods for RS distortion correction rely on a constant velocity assumption and require multiple frames for predicting dense displacement.
EvShutter corrects RS artifacts using a single RGB image and event information with high temporal resolution, employing a novel flow-based deblurring module and a double encoder hourglass network.
EvShutter does not rely on a constant velocity assumption unlike previous methods.
EvShutter employs an event transformation called Filter and Flip (FnF) dedicated to RS to encode only the changes between GS and RS images.

To evaluate the proposed method and facilitate future research, a dataset named RS-ERGB is collected with real events and high-quality RS images, generated from GS images using a simulator based on adaptive interpolation.
The simulator allows the generation of RS images from GS images using adaptive interpolation, enabling the use of inexpensive cameras with long exposure to capture high-quality GS images.

RS correction involves recovering the GS image by estimating the camera motion or dense displacement field during image acquisition and applying motion compensation to align all the pixels to a fixed timestamp.

Gyroscope-based methods utilize a gyro sensor to estimate the rotational motion of the camera. These methods are widely used in mobile devices due to their speed and reliability, but they assume a static scene and a simplified rotational camera motion.
